title: Peach hand pies
subtitle: Individual shortcrust pastry parcels filled with spiced fresh peach.
excerpt: Peach hand pies are small, individual pastry parcels, each one a self-contained serving of shortcrust pastry and spiced peach filling. They bake on a tray without a tin, sealed at the edges with a fork. Each one is roughly the size of a large playing card. Makes 8.
sourceNotes: Hand pies (small, individual pastry parcels without a tin) appear in British baking as pasties and turnovers, in American baking as hand pies and fried pies, and across many other traditions. The format of a D-shaped or square pastry parcel sealed with a fork and baked is a practical one used whenever single-serve portions or portable eating is the goal. The peach filling follows the American summer pie tradition, using fresh stone fruit, sugar, cornflour, and spice. Peaches arrived in British markets in quantity with the expansion of European agricultural exports in the 1980s.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the assembled unbaked hand pies on a tray, then bag. Bake from frozen at 180°C fan for 30 to 35 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry can be made two days ahead and refrigerated. The filling can be made a day ahead.

recipeTools.notes: Two large trays lined with baking paper.

content.text: The filling needs to be cool before it goes into the pastry; hot filling melts the fat and makes the pastry greasy. Make the filling first and let it sit while you make the pastry, or make the filling the day before.

items.prepNote: ripe, stoned, diced into 1 cm pieces

content.text: Combine {{peaches}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{cornflour}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{lemon-juice}} in a bowl. Stir and leave for 15 minutes. The cornflour will absorb the juice. Leave to cool completely.

Rub {{plain-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and cold {{unsalted-butter}} together until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add 4 to 5 tablespoons of cold water, a tablespoon at a time, until the dough comes together. Divide in two, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Heat the oven to 190°C fan (210°C conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper.

Roll one half of the pastry to about 3 mm thick on a floured surface. Cut into 10 cm x 12 cm rectangles (or use a round cutter for a D-shape fold). You need 16 pieces total. Re-roll trimmings as needed.

Place a heaped tablespoon of filling on one half of each pastry piece, leaving a 1.5 cm border. Brush the border with beaten egg. Fold the pastry over to enclose the filling. Press the edges together firmly with a fork to seal. Transfer to the lined trays.
Brush the tops with beaten egg and scatter with {{caster-sugar}}. Cut a small slit in the top of each pie. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until deep golden. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Seal not pressed firmly enough, or filling too wet.
items.fix: Press the fork tines firmly all the way around. A small amount of caramelised leakage is normal. If there is a lot of liquid in the filling before it goes in, drain it first and add a little more cornflour.
Preheat the baking tray in the oven before placing the pies on it. This gives the bottom crust a head start.
